content.text: The Azure CLI (az) is a cross-platform command-line tool for managing Azure resources. It runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and is also pre-installed in Azure Cloud Shell. Every Azure portal action has a CLI equivalent, making the CLI the preferred tool for automation, scripting, and repeatable deployments. This section covers installation on all major platforms and verifying that the installation works.

attrs.alt: Azure CLI installation options by platform: Windows (MSI/WinGet), macOS (Homebrew/pkg), Linux (apt/dnf/script), Docker, and Cloud Shell

content.text: Before You Install

The Azure CLI is a Python application distributed as a standalone installer. The minimum supported versions are Windows 8.1 / Server 2012 R2, macOS 11 (Big Sur), and major Linux distributions from 2018 onward. Python does not need to be pre-installed — the installer bundles its own Python runtime.

If you only need to run occasional commands and cannot or should not install software locally, use Azure Cloud Shell (portal.azure.com > Cloud Shell icon) — it is always on the latest CLI version with no installation required.
Windows
WinGet (Windows Package Manager)

# Open PowerShell or Command Prompt and run:
winget install --exact --id Microsoft.AzureCLI

# Verify:
az version

# Update to the latest version later:
winget upgrade Microsoft.AzureCLI
MSI Installer

# Download and install the current MSI silently via PowerShell:
$ProgressPreference = 'SilentlyContinue'
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri https://aka.ms/installazurecliwindows -OutFile azurecli.msi
Start-Process msiexec.exe -Wait -ArgumentList '/I azurecli.msi /quiet'
Remove-Item azurecli.msi

# Verify:
az version

# Future updates:
# Re-download the MSI from aka.ms/installazurecliwindows and re-run
# OR:
az upgrade
Chocolatey

# If you use Chocolatey package manager:
choco install azure-cli

# Update:
choco upgrade azure-cli
macOS
Homebrew (Recommended)

# Install Homebrew first if not already installed:
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

# Install Azure CLI:
brew update && brew install azure-cli

# Verify:
az version

# Update later:
brew update && brew upgrade azure-cli
PKG Installer

# Download the .pkg file from:
# https://aka.ms/installazureclimac
# Double-click to run the installer wizard.

# Or via curl (installs to /usr/local/bin/az):
curl -L https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCli | bash

# Verify:
az version

# Update via the same curl command or brew if you switch to Homebrew
Linux
Ubuntu and Debian (apt)

# One-line install script (sets up the Microsoft package repo and installs):
curl -sL https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCLIDeb | sudo bash

# Verify:
az version

# Manual step-by-step (same result, more transparency):
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install ca-certificates curl apt-transport-https lsb-release gnupg

# Add Microsoft signing key
sudo mkdir -p /etc/apt/keyrings
curl -sLS https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc | \
  gpg --dearmor | sudo tee /etc/apt/keyrings/microsoft.gpg > /dev/null
sudo chmod go+r /etc/apt/keyrings/microsoft.gpg

# Add the Azure CLI repository
AZ_DIST=$(lsb_release -cs)
echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/microsoft.gpg] \
  https://packages.microsoft.com/repos/azure-cli/ $AZ_DIST main" | \
  sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/azure-cli.list

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install azure-cli

# Update later:
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade azure-cli
RHEL, Fedora, and CentOS (dnf / yum)

# Import the Microsoft GPG key:
sudo rpm --import https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc

# Add the repository:
sudo dnf install -y https://packages.microsoft.com/config/rhel/9.0/packages-microsoft-prod.rpm

# Install:
sudo dnf install azure-cli

# Verify:
az version

# Update later:
sudo dnf update azure-cli

# For CentOS 7 / RHEL 7 (yum):
sudo yum install azure-cli
Any Linux Distro (Bash Script)

# Universal install script — works on any distro with bash and Python 3.6+:
curl -L https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCli | bash

# The script installs to $HOME/bin/az by default.
# Add it to PATH if not already there:
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

# Verify:
az version
Docker

Microsoft publishes an official Azure CLI Docker image. This is useful in CI/CD pipelines, ephemeral build environments, and when you want to test a specific CLI version without affecting your local install.

# Run an interactive Azure CLI session in Docker:
docker run -it mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli

# Inside the container, sign in and run commands:
az login
az account list -o table
az vm list -o table

# Run a single command without interactive mode:
docker run mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli az version

# Pin to a specific CLI version (find tags at mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli):
docker run -it mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli:2.60.0

# Mount a local directory to persist files across container runs:
docker run -it \
  -v "$HOME/.azure:/root/.azure" \
  mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli
# Mounts credentials directory so you don't need to az login on every run
CI/CD Environments
Most CI/CD platforms either pre-install the Azure CLI or provide a first-class action/task to install it. You authenticate using a service principal or workload identity, not interactive login.
GitHub Actions

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy to Azure

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Login using a service principal stored as a secret
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          creds: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CREDENTIALS }}

      # Azure CLI is now available for subsequent steps
      - name: Check subscription
        run: az account show -o table

      - name: Deploy Bicep template
        run: |
          az deployment group create \
            --resource-group rg-prod \
            --template-file main.bicep \
            --parameters @params.prod.json
Azure DevOps Pipelines

# azure-pipelines.yml
trigger:
  - main

pool:
  vmImage: ubuntu-latest

steps:
  - task: AzureCLI@2
    displayName: Deploy via Azure CLI
    inputs:
      azureSubscription: my-service-connection    # configured in DevOps project settings
      scriptType: bash
      scriptLocation: inlineScript
      inlineScript: |
        az account show -o table
        az deployment group create \
          --resource-group rg-prod \
          --template-file main.bicep \
          --parameters @params.prod.json
Verifying the Installation

# Check version:
az version
# Output: JSON with "azure-cli" version, Python version, and extensions

# Check version concisely:
az version --query '"azure-cli"' -o tsv

# Sign in interactively (opens browser):
az login

# Sign in to a specific tenant:
az login --tenant contoso.onmicrosoft.com

# Sign in with a service principal (for automation):
az login \
  --service-principal \
  --username "$APP_ID" \
  --password "$CLIENT_SECRET" \
  --tenant "$TENANT_ID"

# Confirm the signed-in account and default subscription:
az account show -o table

# List all accessible subscriptions:
az account list -o table
Updating the Azure CLI
Microsoft releases Azure CLI updates frequently. The built-in upgrade command works on all platforms (except when installed via a system package manager that manages its own updates).

# Built-in upgrade command (works on most platforms):
az upgrade

# Platform-specific update commands:
# macOS (Homebrew):
brew update && brew upgrade azure-cli

# Ubuntu/Debian:
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade azure-cli

# RHEL/Fedora:
sudo dnf update azure-cli

# Windows (WinGet):
winget upgrade Microsoft.AzureCLI

# Check if an update is available without installing:
az version --query '"azure-cli"' -o tsv
# Compare with: https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/releases/latest
Uninstalling the Azure CLI

# macOS (Homebrew):
brew uninstall azure-cli

# Ubuntu/Debian:
sudo apt-get remove -y azure-cli
sudo apt-get autoremove

# RHEL/Fedora:
sudo dnf remove azure-cli

# Windows: Control Panel > Add/Remove Programs > "Microsoft Azure CLI"
# Or via WinGet:
winget uninstall Microsoft.AzureCLI

# Remove cached credentials after uninstall:
rm -rf ~/.azure
Common Installation Issues
Issue
Cause
Fix
'az' not found after install (Windows)
PATH not updated in the current shell
Close and reopen PowerShell/Command Prompt; or log out and back in
'az' not found after install (Linux/macOS)
Shell config not sourced
Run: source ~/.bashrc or source ~/.zshrc
SSL certificate errors during install
Corporate proxy intercepting TLS
Set HTTPS_PROXY env var; install proxy's CA cert
Permission denied on Linux
Missing sudo
Prefix commands with sudo
curl script fails on minimal Docker image
curl or bash not installed
Install curl/bash first: apt-get install -y curl bash
Old CLI version cached in CI
CI runner caching the pre-installed version
Add 'az upgrade' step or pin the Azure CLI action version explicitly
Shell Completion
The Azure CLI can register tab-completion for commands and resource names in Bash, Zsh, and Fish. This significantly reduces typing in day-to-day use.

# Enable tab completion for Bash:
# The CLI ships a completion script at:
# $(az --version 2>&1 | grep "^azure-cli" | head -1)
# Standard setup:
az completion bash >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

# Verify: type "az " and press Tab — command categories appear
# Type "az vm " and press Tab — vm sub-commands appear

# Enable for Zsh:
az completion zsh >> ~/.zshrc
source ~/.zshrc

# Enable for Fish:
az completion fish > ~/.config/fish/completions/az.fish

# After enabling, completion works for:
# - Command groups:       az vm, az storage, az network ...
# - Sub-commands:        az vm create, az vm list, az vm start ...
# - Flag names:          --resource-group, --location, --name ...
# - Some flag values:    --location (region names), --sku (VM sizes)
Configuring the Azure CLI
The CLI stores configuration in ~/.azure/config. You can set default values for commonly used flags so you do not have to repeat them on every command.

# Set a default resource group (eliminates --resource-group flag):
az configure --defaults group=rg-prod

# Set a default location:
az configure --defaults location=eastus

# Set a default output format (table, json, tsv, yaml, jsonc, none):
az configure --defaults output=table

# View current configuration:
az configure --list-defaults

# Remove a default:
az configure --defaults group=''

# Config file location (edit directly if needed):
cat ~/.azure/config
# Example config file contents:
# [defaults]
# group = rg-prod
# location = eastus
# output = table

# Per-command override — explicit flags always override defaults:
az vm list --output json   # overrides the 'table' default for this run
Environment Variable Overrides

# Environment variables override config file defaults:
export AZURE_DEFAULTS_GROUP=rg-staging
export AZURE_DEFAULTS_LOCATION=westeurope

# Now az commands use rg-staging without --resource-group

# Useful in CI/CD pipelines to inject environment-specific defaults:
# .github/workflows/deploy-staging.yml
# env:
#   AZURE_DEFAULTS_GROUP: rg-staging
#   AZURE_DEFAULTS_LOCATION: westeurope

# Other useful environment variables:
AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID   # default subscription (alternative to az account set)
AZURE_TENANT_ID         # target tenant for service principal login
AZURE_CLIENT_ID         # service principal app ID
AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET     # service principal secret
# When these three are set, 'az login' is not needed — the CLI auto-authenticates
Installing CLI Extensions
Many Azure services have CLI extensions that add commands not included in the base CLI installation. Extensions are installed on demand and managed with 'az extension'.

# List all available extensions:
az extension list-available -o table

# Install a specific extension:
az extension add --name azure-devops
az extension add --name aks-preview
az extension add --name application-insights
az extension add --name ssh

# List installed extensions:
az extension list -o table

# Update all installed extensions:
az extension update --all

# Remove an extension:
az extension remove --name azure-devops

# Common extensions and what they add:
# azure-devops      → az devops, az boards, az pipelines, az repos
# aks-preview       → preview AKS features not yet in GA CLI
# ssh               → az ssh vm (connect to VMs via SSH through Azure AD)
# application-insights → az monitor app-insights
# containerapp      → az containerapp (Azure Container Apps)
# connectedmachine  → az connectedmachine (Azure Arc)
Multiple CLI Installations and Version Pinning
In teams with mixed environments or when testing CLI upgrades, it can be useful to run multiple CLI versions side by side. The recommended approach is Docker.

# Run a specific CLI version without changing your local install:
docker run -it mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli:2.59.0 bash
# Inside: az version  → 2.59.0

docker run -it mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli:2.60.0 bash
# Inside: az version  → 2.60.0

# Compare output between CLI versions for a command:
docker run mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli:2.59.0 az vm list-skus --location eastus -o table > v259.txt
docker run mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli:2.60.0 az vm list-skus --location eastus -o table > v260.txt
diff v259.txt v260.txt

# Pin the CLI version in a Dockerfile for reproducible builds:
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli:2.60.0
RUN az extension add --name aks-preview
ENTRYPOINT ["az"]
Proxies and Corporate Environments
In corporate networks with an HTTP/HTTPS proxy, the CLI needs proxy settings to reach Azure endpoints. If the proxy performs TLS inspection, the proxy's CA certificate must also be trusted.

# Set proxy for the current shell session:
export HTTP_PROXY=http://proxy.contoso.com:8080
export HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.contoso.com:8080
export NO_PROXY=localhost,127.0.0.1,*.internal.contoso.com

# Persist proxy settings in ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
echo 'export HTTP_PROXY=http://proxy.contoso.com:8080' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'export HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.contoso.com:8080' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

# Trust a corporate CA certificate (Linux):
sudo cp /path/to/corporate-ca.crt /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/
sudo update-ca-certificates

# Trust a corporate CA certificate (macOS):
security add-trusted-cert -d -r trustRoot \
  -k /Library/Keychains/System.keychain /path/to/corporate-ca.crt

# Tell the Azure CLI to use the system certificate store:
az config set core.certs=/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
# Or set via environment variable:
export REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE=/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt

# Test connectivity after proxy configuration:
az account show
Summary

Install the Azure CLI via the platform's native package manager: WinGet or MSI on Windows, Homebrew or pkg on macOS, apt or dnf on Linux. Docker and Cloud Shell require no local installation. After installing, run 'az login' to authenticate and 'az version' to confirm the installation. Keep the CLI current with 'az upgrade' or the platform's package manager — Azure releases updates every few weeks. In CI/CD pipelines, authenticate with a service principal rather than interactive login, using the azure/login GitHub Action or the AzureCLI@2 Azure DevOps task.
